channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: AI News: Mistral is back, DeepSeek owns AI.com, Sam's 3 Laws of AI
executive_summary: Mistral launched a fast AI model that runs on Swiss silicon and requires no account, competing with GPT-4 in speed. ChatGPT is now the sixth most visited website, capturing 2.3% of global web traffic, just before OpenAI's Super Bowl ad. DeepSeek has taken ownership of AI.com from Google, indicating a shift in AI branding power.
bullets: Mistral launched a consumer AI model that runs on Swiss silicon and requires no account, making it incredibly accessible. This model competes with GPT-4 in terms of intelligence but is designed for lightning-fast performance. Imagine a sports car that can go from 0 to 60 mph in seconds without needing to fill out paperwork.

ChatGPT now accounts for 2.3% of all internet traffic, making it the sixth most visited website globally. This level of traffic indicates its popularity and influence in the AI space, especially as it grows even more with upcoming marketing efforts. Think of it like a blockbuster movie that draws in millions of viewers, setting the stage for future sequels.

OpenAI’s Super Bowl ad emphasized waiting dots, which some found to be an odd branding choice, especially given Mistral’s speed advantage. In contrast, Google’s Gemini ad showcased real-world AI applications, demonstrating practical uses of AI technology. It's like a chef showcasing a beautifully plated dish while another chef just shows the ingredients sitting on a counter.

DeepSeek now owns the domain AI.com, which previously belonged to Google, marking a significant shift in AI branding. This change could influence how consumers perceive AI companies and their products. Think of it like a new restaurant taking over a famous location, potentially attracting a whole new crowd of diners.

Sam Altman, CEO of OpenAI, outlined three laws of AI, stating that the cost of a given level of artificial intelligence drops roughly 10x every year. This means that as technology advances, it becomes cheaper to access powerful AI tools, making them more available to everyone. Imagine a smartphone that costs $1,000 today but will only cost $100 in a few years—suddenly, everyone can afford one.

Altman also noted that more compute power leads to more intelligence, but this relationship is logarithmic. This means that doubling the resources doesn’t double the intelligence gained, but rather increases it at a slower rate. Think of it like trying to fill a bathtub with water; adding more water doesn’t necessarily fill it twice as fast if the drain is still open.

Intelligence has super-exponential value, which justifies OpenAI’s significant spending on AI development. This means that as AI becomes smarter, its value increases dramatically, making investments in AI worthwhile. It's like investing in a startup that suddenly becomes the next big tech giant—your initial investment pays off exponentially.

The competition in AI is intensifying, with Mistral's speed, DeepSeek's emergence, and OpenAI's aggressive scaling strategies. This arms race means that companies must innovate quickly to stay relevant and competitive. Imagine a high-stakes race where every second counts, and the lead can change in an instant.

Mistral's model is designed to be user-friendly, requiring no account setup, which is a significant advantage in attracting users. This ease of access can lead to rapid adoption and a larger user base. Think of it like a theme park that lets you in for free on opening day—everyone wants to experience the fun without any barriers.

OpenAI's Super Bowl ad was released just before ChatGPT became the sixth most visited website, indicating a strategic push to capitalize on its growing popularity. This timing could amplify interest and user engagement further. It's like launching a new product during the holiday season when everyone is already in the shopping mood.

The shift of AI.com from Google to DeepSeek suggests a potential change in the landscape of AI branding and ownership. This could lead to new strategies for how companies position themselves in the market. Think of it like a famous athlete switching teams, creating buzz and altering the dynamics of the league.

Sam Altman's observations highlight the rapid evolution of AI and the need for companies to adapt quickly to maintain their competitive edge. Ignoring these trends could leave companies behind in a fast-paced industry. It's like a runner who doesn't train for a marathon—without preparation, they risk falling far behind the competition.
key_takeaways: Monitor Mistral's developments closely, as their fast AI model could set new standards in the industry. If you ignore this, you might miss out on understanding emerging trends that could shape your career.
Prepare for the upcoming changes in AI branding, especially with DeepSeek's acquisition of AI.com. This shift could affect how you position your own projects or products in the market.
Stay updated on Sam Altman's three laws of AI, particularly the 10x cost reduction each year. If you don't, you may miss opportunities to leverage cheaper AI tools as they become available.
Consider how the competition in AI is accelerating and be ready to adapt your strategies accordingly. Ignoring this could leave you unprepared for the rapidly changing landscape of AI technology.

technical_terms.Mistral: A French AI company known for its fast consumer AI model that runs on Swiss silicon. Think of it like a high-speed train that can take you to your destination faster than any other option.
technical_terms.ChatGPT: An AI chatbot developed by OpenAI that has become one of the most visited websites globally. Like a popular café that everyone wants to visit for its unique offerings.
technical_terms.DeepSeek: A company that now owns the domain AI.com, previously controlled by Google, indicating a shift in AI branding power. Think of it as a new player stepping into a well-established game, changing the rules.
technical_terms.Super Bowl ad: A high-profile advertisement aired during the Super Bowl, which is a major event for marketing. Like a grand stage where brands showcase their best performances to millions of viewers.
technical_terms.Sam Altman: The CEO of OpenAI who has outlined key principles regarding AI development and investment. Think of him as a coach guiding a team through the complexities of a championship season.
technical_terms.AI.com: A domain that serves as a significant online presence for AI-related content, now owned by DeepSeek. Like a prime piece of real estate in a bustling city, attracting attention and traffic.
technical_terms.AI arms race: The competitive landscape among companies to develop and deploy AI technologies rapidly. Think of it like a race where every competitor is trying to outpace the others to claim victory.
technical_terms.Logarithmic relationship: A mathematical concept where doubling input does not double output, but increases it at a slower rate. Like trying to fill a balloon; the more air you blow in, the harder it gets to add more air without bursting.
technical_terms.Super-exponential value: A concept where the value of intelligence increases dramatically as it becomes more advanced. Think of it like a snowball rolling down a hill, growing larger and faster as it picks up more snow.
technical_terms.Compute power: The amount of processing capability available for running AI algorithms. Like the horsepower of a car; the more horsepower, the faster and more efficiently it can go.
technical_terms.Consumer model: An AI product designed for everyday users without complex requirements. Think of it like a smartphone that anyone can use without needing to understand the technology behind it.
technical_terms.Branding power: The influence and recognition a company has in the market based on its name and products. Like a famous brand of shoes that everyone wants to wear because of its reputation.
